California
Fair Political
Practices Commission SUPER5ED
OL,I
9
’7Z.1aX4)
Michael C.
Moss
4293 Hiliview Drive
Pittsburg,
CA 94565
Dear Mr.
Moss:
April 4,
1994
Re:
Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-94-100
We respond to your request for advice regarding the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”),
1
and its application to you as a director of the Los Medanos
Community Hospital District Board of Directors.
QUESTIONS
PRESENTED
1.
Are you subject to the conflict of interest provisions
of the Act as a member of the board of directors of a local
hospital district?
2.
Does your spouse’s employment in the district’s
hospital,
by itself, raise a conflict under the Act?
3.
Should you recuse yourself from actions and decisions
which directly or indirectly affect your spouse’s continued
employment with the hospital?
CONCLUSION
1.
Yes.
A local hospital district is a government agency.
Therefore,
a director would be a public .o.fficial subject to the
provisions of the Act.
2.
No.
The mere fact that your spouse is employed in the
hospital regulated by your board does not present a conflict under
the Act.
However,
under certain circumstances,
more fully
described below,
a conflict may arise.
3.
Yes.
Under the Act you should not make, participate in
making or use your official position to influence decisions
concerning the hiring, firing,
demotion or disciplining of your
spouse.
1
Government Code Sections 8lOOO-9lO15
All statutory
are to the Government Code unless otherise indicated.
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
references
Commission
Sections
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94-100
Page
2
FACTS
You are a director of the Los Medanos Community Hospital
District Board.
You have been a director since 1987 and your
present term expires in October 1994.
Your wife has worked for
the hospital since 1979 as a part-time ward clerk.
The hospital
district has defaulted on its bond obligations.
Future decisions
of the board may include the sale, merger or affiliation of the
hospital.
These decisions could result in a restructuring which
may lead to the elimination of hospital positions,
including your
wife’s part-time position.
ANALYSIS
A local hospital district, created under the authority of
California Health and Safety Code Sections 32000 et seq., is a
public entity.
(Gallegos Advice Letter,
No.
1-91—047).
As a
director of the Los Medanos Community Hospital District,
you would
be a public official subject to the provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87100 contains the general prohibition on public
officials making or participating in the making of decisions in
which they have a conflict of interest.
Section 87100 provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to.
know he has a financial interest.
“Financial interest” is
a defined term under the Act and
includes any source of income of two hundred and fifty dollars or
more received within the twelve months prior to any decision.
(Section 87103(c).)
Generally,
a public official’s community
property interest in his or her spouse’s--income would be
considered income to the public official.
(Section 82030(a).)
However,
income and reimbursement for expenses from a state,
local,
or federal government agency is expressly excluded from the
definition of income.
(Section 82030(b) (2).)
Accordingly, the income your spouse receives as an employee
of the hospital does not,
by itself, present a conflict of
interest which would prohibit you from exercising your official
duties as a director of the hospital district. ( Campbell
Advice Letter,
No.
A-94-002
(School district board member has no
conflict of interest merely because spouse is employed as a
teacher in district school).)
However,
under Commission
regulations, certain decisions affecting your spouse’s employment
would create a conflict of interest,
anci you would be prohibited
from making or participating in making uOh decisions.
Under Commission regulations,
a conflict of interest may
still exist where a decision will result in the hiring, firing,
promotion,
demotion or disciplining of an official’s spouse which
results in the personal expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities
of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or


File No.
A—94—l00
Page
3
decreasing by two hundred and fifty dollars or more.
(Regulations
18702.1(a) (4)
and 18702.1(c) (1); Torrance Advice Letter,
No.
1—89-142;
Schreiber Advice Letter,
No.
A—88—459).
It is
likely that a decision to terminate your wife’s position with the
hospital will affect your family’s expenses,
income, assets or
liabilities in an amount greater than two hundred and fifty
dollars.
Therefore,
under the regulations as presently
interpreted,
you would be obligated to recuse yourself from making
or participating in making any decision which could have the
reasonably foreseeable result of eliminating your wife’s position
as a hospital employee.
2
In clarifying this prohibition in previous advice letters,
the Commission has stated that a public official may participate
in collective bargaining decisions concerning a spouse’s
bargaining unit so long as the decision affects all employees in
the same job classification in the same manner
(Campbell Advice
Letter,
No.
A-94-002),
but a public official may not participate
in decisions which lead directly to the hiring or firing of a
spouse (Schreiber Advice Letter,
No.
A—88-459 and Torrance Advice
Letter,
No.
1—89-142).
We have enclosed copies of these letters
for your review.
In summary, while the mere fact that your wife works for the
hospital controlled by the district board on which you serve does
not,
by itself, present a conflict, the Act does prohibit you from
making, participating in the making,
or using your official
position to influence decisions which may result in the hiring,
firing, promotion,
demotion or disciplining of your spouse.
Additionally,
you may not make, participate in making,
or use your
official position to influence any decision which would set a
salary for your spouse which is different from salaries paid to
other employees in your spouse’s job classification or position.
We hope that this letter fully addresses the concerns you
have regarding your public duties.
If you have any further
questions or wish to seek additional advice,
you may contact this
agency at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Couns’’i
3
By2E{a1l&
Counsel,
Legal Division
2
Whether the financial consequencesbf a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a goiernmenta1 decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198).
